“The contract market isn’t cheaper. It’s sharper.”

We’re seeing day rates for:
CTOs hit £2,900
CPOs pushing £3,100
AI talent commanding premium pricing
 
But the real shift isn’t cost
It’s expectation

Businesses aren’t hiring contractors to fill gaps anymore
They’re hiring them to deliver outcomes

If you can’t offer:
Clear scope
Fast onboarding
Immediate impact
 
You won’t access the best talent

Download the full UK Contract Salary Survey here: https://lnkd.in/eBA5GbKa

Hiring | Mid-level Dynamics 365 Developer 

Location: Mumbai, Banglore, Hyderabad, Chennai, Indore, Bhopal, Gurugram, Noida 
Work Mode: Work from Office 
Years of Exp: 5+ Years 
Duration: 6 Months Contract (Extendable)

Job Summary:
We are seeking a Mid-level Dynamics 365 Developer for one of our leading client with foundational experience in Dynamics 365 Customer Service and interest in Microsoft Copilot / AI capabilities. The role focuses on supporting development activities, learning best practices, and contributing to solution delivery under the guidance of senior team members in an Agile environment.

Key Responsibilities:
• Support development and customization of Dynamics 365 Customer Service solutions
• Configure entities, forms, views, workflows, and business rules
• Assist in implementing Copilot AI-powered features and enhancements
• Build and support solutions using Power Platform including Power Apps, Power Automate, Power Pages
• Support integrations and data flows with guidance from senior developers
• Participate in Agile ceremonies including sprint planning, reviews, and retrospectives
• Assist in testing, documentation, and defect resolution
• Follow coding standards, security models, and best practices

Required Qualifications
• Bachelor’s degree in Computer Science, IT, or related field
• Microsoft Dynamics 365 or Power Platform certification
• 5+ years of experience in Dynamics 365 CE or Customer Service
• Basic understanding of Dataverse, security roles, and case management
• Exposure to Power Platform tools
• Willingness to learn and work with AI-driven features like Dynamics 365 Copilot

NOTE: Need solid candidates, with UAN number and Linkedin profile. Freelancers won't be considered.

Interested professionals can connect or share profiles and references on
yashvi.bid@bolt-techservices.com & 
shraddha.parab@bolt-techservices.com

The CFO who says yes to everything is not a strategic partner. They're a liability.

CFO.com published a piece this week called "Why 2026 will reward CFOs who say yes."

The argument is that the winning finance chiefs this year will embrace visibility, AI, broader leadership mandates, and a more expansive definition of their role. Say yes to evolution. Say yes to learning. Say yes to technology and growth.

I agree with about half of it.

The half I disagree with is the framing.

Because the CFO's structural value in any organisation is not their willingness to say yes. It's their willingness to say no. Or more precisely: it's their willingness to say "the numbers don't support this" in a room where everyone else has already decided they want to do it.

That's an uncomfortable thing to be. I've been that person. The one who tables the cash flow projection that shows the hiring plan is six months ahead of the revenue curve. The one who asks what the gross margin looks like when the sales team is celebrating a big new contract. The one who flags the board pack narrative that has quietly drifted away from what the actuals actually show.

None of those conversations make you popular in the moment.

But they are the conversations that make a CFO worth having in the room.

The risk with the "say yes" framing is that it conflates agility with agreeableness. A CFO should absolutely evolve, adopt new tools, build a public voice, and operate across a broader strategic remit. I do all of those things and I think they matter. But that evolution is only valuable if the underlying judgment remains independent.

The moment a CFO starts saying yes to everything, they've traded in the one thing that makes them different from everyone else at the table.

The CEO has commercial pressure pulling them toward optimism. The sales team has quota. The founder has vision and ego and four years of sunken cost in an idea. The CFO has the numbers, and the obligation to say what the numbers say, even when it's not what the room wants to hear.

2026 does reward CFOs who evolve. But the ones who will matter most are not the most agreeable ones.

They're the ones who've stayed honest.

#CFO #FinanceLeadership #Leadership

🚀 What Top Companies Are Really Hiring for in Senior Java Full Stack Developers (2026 Market Insights)
After spending 10+ years building enterprise applications, one thing is very clear in today’s market—
the bar for “Senior” has significantly evolved.
It’s no longer about knowing Java, Spring Boot, or a frontend framework.
Recruiters and hiring managers are now looking for engineers who can design, scale, and own complex systems end-to-end.
Here’s what’s truly driving hiring decisions right now 👇

🔹 1. Microservices + Event-Driven Architecture
Companies are prioritizing developers who can build scalable, loosely coupled systems using Spring Boot and tools like Kafka or RabbitMQ.
Real-time processing and system resilience are key.

🔹 2. Cloud-Native Development is Mandatory
Hands-on experience with AWS, Azure, or GCP, along with Docker & Kubernetes, is expected.
It’s not just deployment—it's about designing for scalability, fault tolerance, and cost efficiency.

🔹 3. AI-Ready Application Development
A major shift in 2026—organizations are integrating AI into their platforms.
Developers who can extend Java applications with AI/LLM capabilities are getting strong attention.

🔹 4. Full Stack Ownership (Backend + Frontend)
Senior developers are expected to go beyond APIs—
➡️ Build robust backend services
➡️ Integrate seamlessly with Angular/React
➡️ Deliver complete user-focused solutions

🔹 5. DevOps + CI/CD Mindset
The expectation today is simple:
You build it, you deploy it, you support it.
Experience with Jenkins, Terraform, Git pipelines, and monitoring tools is highly valued.

🔹 6. Business Impact & Problem Solving
What truly differentiates candidates now is the ability to:
✔️ Make architectural decisions
✔️ Optimize performance at scale
✔️ Solve real business problems—not just write code

💡 Key Takeaway:
The market isn’t just competitive—it’s selective.
The most in-demand developers are those who combine:
✅ Strong Java & Microservices foundation
✅ Cloud + Containerization expertise
✅ Event-driven and real-time system experience
✅ AI awareness and adaptability
✅ End-to-end ownership mindset

Yesterday I was grateful to be invited to and attend MMC Ventures's meet-up, focused on Deploying AI Agents.

We heard from brilliant speakers: Sevi U. (MMC Ventures) Bill Leaver (Synthesia), Romain Sestier (StackOne), Henry Comfort (Geordie AI), and Nick Carmont (Mistral AI).

My main takeaways from the night:

💡The demo-to-production gap is still huge: The impressive bit is easy. Getting an agent to work reliably in a messy real-world environment, with real users, real data, and real compliance requirements (the hard bit!), is a completely different problem.

💡 The autonomy/control trade-off is the actual product decision: The winning products won't be the most autonomous. They'll be the ones enterprises actually trust, which means visibility into what the agent is doing, what it can access, and how to pull the brakes.

💡 Forward deployment is massively underrated: The bottleneck isn't model quality, it's context. Extracting real workflow knowledge from the people doing the job (not the buyers signing the contract) is still manual, still hard, and still one of the most defensible things a startup can build.

💡 Horizontal is crowded: Vertical is the opportunity. Enterprises are defaulting to incumbents for general-purpose tooling. Startups win when they're specific, benchmark-driven, and genuinely better at one painful workflow than any platform product could be.

💡 Technology is improving faster than ROI is being captured: The next breakout AI companies will probably look less like model wrappers and more like workflow specialists; narrow focus, strong evals, enterprise-grade trust.

From a hiring perspective, two things jumped out:

👉🏻 The forward deployment point maps directly onto something I see in searches right now: founders struggling to articulate what "good" looks like for an early AI engineer hire, because the actual value isn't in the model, it's in the workflow context around it. The engineers who can extract that knowledge and turn it into something production-ready are the most sought after right now.

👉🏻 And the vertical vs horizontal point has a talent equivalent: Engineers moving into start-ups want to see defensibility from the likes of OpenAI and Claude releases.

I spoke with some awesome investors, founders and operators - a room full of brilliant individuals. Congrats to Georgia Higgs and Advika Jalan for hosting a great event 👏

Imagine walking into a bank and demanding a ₹1,358 crore loan.

No collateral. 0% interest. And you tell them you’ll pay it back in "services" whenever you get around to it.

The bank would laugh you out of the building. But for Info Edge (Naukri.com)? That’s just a standard Tuesday. 💼

That figure now stands at a massive ₹1,358 crore—up 19.3% YoY. 📈

Why is this the ultimate financial flex?

In simple terms, this is cash paid upfront by customers for services not yet rendered. It’s a "float" that acts as a lethal strategic weapon:

Interest-Free Fuel: While other tech startups burn equity to buy runway, Info Edge is effectively borrowing from its own customers. For free.

The Ultimate Shield: The global hiring market is "modest" right now. 

Recruitment cycles are slowing. But this pre-booked mountain of cash insulates them from the chaos.

Funding the Future: This safety net allows management to ignore quarterly noise. They are betting BIG on a dedicated AI team and the 'Naukri Talent Cloud' because the bill is already paid.

The Real Takeaway:
Customers don't lock in multi-year contracts and tie up significant capital unless there is deep trust. Info Edge hasn't just built a platform; they’ve built a financial fortress.

Most companies have a product. Info Edge has a moat made of ₹1,358 crore in "pre-paid" loyalty. 🏰

Did you know the "Balance Sheet" held this much power?

Most in-house legal teams have bolted AI onto a 2015 org chart and called it transformation.

I say this as someone who works in an in-house team and also builds software. I'm not throwing stones.
  
But here's what I'm seeing: the vast majority of in-house legal functions have adopted AI in the same way: a Copilot-style tool that sits alongside the existing team, speeds up drafting, helps with research, maybe summarises long contracts.
  
That's augmentation. It's useful. It's not transformation.
  
The structure underneath is unchanged. The same hierarchy. The same workflow. Legal as a function that reacts to the business rather than anticipates it. The same bottlenecks, just slightly faster.
  
The clearance agent conversation I wrote about applies directly here.
  
When you genuinely reimagine an in-house legal department, the shape of it changes completely. Routine contract review largely disappears as a human task. First-draft advice, same. What's left, and what grows, is judgment, strategy, and governance.
  
But here's the issue: most legal teams are planning for the augmented version, not the reimagined one. And those require very different hiring decisions, very different capability investment, very different structures.
  
The organisations that figure this out first won't just have more efficient legal teams. They'll have a genuine competitive advantage in how they manage risk.
  
I'll write about what those new roles actually look like next week.

#InHouseLegal #LegalTech #FutureOfWork

You want an HR AI Expert that combines policy + process + system + compliance. That’s what gets adoption and budget.

⸻

🧠 1. WHAT YOUR KNOWLEDGE BASE SHOULD ACTUALLY INCLUDE

⚖️ A. UK Employment Law (baseline, not the product)

Include:
	•	Employment Rights Act 1996
	•	Equality Act 2010
	•	Working Time Regulations 1998
	•	National Minimum Wage Act 1998
	•	Data Protection Act 2018

👉 But don’t stop here. This alone won’t sell.

⸻

🏢 B. COMPANY HR POLICIES (this is where value is)

This is your gold:
	•	Leave policies (annual, sick, parental)
	•	Disciplinary procedures
	•	Grievance processes
	•	Remote working rules
	•	Expense policies
	•	Onboarding / offboarding checklists

👉 Why this matters:
Employees don’t ask “what does the law say?”
They ask:

“Can I carry over leave?”
“What happens if I’m off sick?”

⸻

⚙️ C. HR SYSTEM KNOWLEDGE (your edge)

Tie it to:
	•	PeopleSoft
	•	SAP SuccessFactors

Include:
	•	How to hire
	•	How to update job data
	•	How to request leave
	•	How to fix issues
      •     Include Precedents 

👉 This is what removes support tickets.

⸻

📊 D. HR PROCESSES (make it actionable)
	•	Hiring workflows
	•	Promotions
	•	Performance reviews
	•	Terminations
	•	Payroll cycles

👉 Turn these into step-by-step playbooks.

⸻

🧾 E. DOCUMENTS & TEMPLATES
	•	Contracts
	•	Offer letters
	•	Disciplinary letters
	•	Warning templates
	•	Exit forms

👉 Your AI should generate these instantly.

⸻

🚨 F. RISK & COMPLIANCE (this is your premium upsell)
	•	GDPR handling of employee data
	•	AI usage policies
	•	Audit trails
	•	Legal disclaimers 

ACCESS MODEL (THIS IS WHERE YOU NEED TO DECIDE)

You asked a very important question here. Most people mess this up.

⸻

OPTION 1. Give ALL employees access

✅ Pros:
	•	Reduces HR tickets massively
	•	High adoption
	•	Easy to sell

❌ Cons:
	•	Risk of misuse
	•	Needs guardrails
	•	Must be very simple

👉 Use for:
	•	Policy questions
	•	Leave queries
	•	Basic HR processes

⸻

OPTION 2. Intranet + AI Bot (BEST MODEL)

👉 This is the one you want.

Structure:
	•	HR portal (SharePoint / website)
	•	Knowledge base pages
	•	AI Expert in bottom corner

Users:
	•	Browse → read
	•	Ask → clarify

👉 Why this wins:
	•	Controlled environment
	•	Context-aware answers
	•	Scalable

⸻

OPTION 3. HR-only / power users

👉 Use this for:
	•	Sensitive processes
	•	Legal scenarios
	•	Data-heavy tasks

⸻

🔥 RECOMMENDED SETUP (DO THIS)

Don’t overthink it. Do this:

Layer 1. Employee AI
	•	Leave
	•	Policies
	•	Basic HR questions

Layer 2. Manager AI
	•	Hiring
	•	Performance
	•	Disciplinary guidance

Layer 3. HR AI (premium)
	•	Legal risk
	•	Complex cases
	•	Data + system fixes

⸻

💡 WHAT YOU’RE REALLY BUILDING

Not:
“An HR chatbot”

But:

👉 “A 24/7 HR Operations Engine that replaces repetitive HR work”

This can be set up in about 30 minutes. DM me or book a call to discuss options.

If a senior leader builds personal AI capability that creates measurable value inside their company, how should that be treated?

I had a fascinating conversation recently with a C-suite leader.
Over time, they’ve built their own AI agents, personally.
Not company tools.
Not part of the tech stack.
Something they’ve developed in their own time, that now allows them to:
– Test and shape new functions before hiring
– Pressure-test investment decisions
– Do work that would normally require a team
The impact is real.
Not in “jobs replaced”, but in better decisions, clearer roles, and more effective investment of headcount. The real outcome a potential saving for the business of a significant value. 
But it raises a more difficult question:
If a senior leader builds capability like this themselves, and it creates measurable commercial value inside the business…who actually owns that value?
Because right now, there’s no clear framework:
– It can’t easily sit inside the company (it exposes the individual’s IP)
– It can’t fully sit outside (it may rely on, or need, company data)
– And unlike consultants, there’s no structure governing how this is used
Feels like leadership capability is evolving faster than the contracts around it.
Curious how others are thinking about this. Many thanks in advance.

I have a standing set of questions I ask in every one-on-one right now when trying to evaluate the use of AI. They are always the same and focus on the work itself.

1/ What takes up most of your time while requiring the least judgment? 
2/ Where in your work do you wish you had more time to think?  
3/ If someone with deep AI expertise sat next to you every day, what would you ask them to take on?

I focus on these questions because they surface how work actually happens. Asking about tools often yields surface-level answers. People describe what they have tried. These questions bring the conversation back to the work itself and where time is being spent.

Across a team of 35 people, patterns become clear very quickly. Certain tasks appear again and again, even across different roles. We implemented AI to improve quality and process in several areas: in hiring, using AI as a first interviewer to assess role fit and cultural alignment; in legal, to speed up NDA and contract review time for sales; and in marketing, to customize guides and content based on customer type, problem, and compelling event.

What people say gives you a clearer picture of how their time is being spent. You start to see where effort goes into work that does not require much judgment, and where people feel pulled in too many directions. That makes it easier to decide where AI can be introduced in a way that connects directly to the day-to-day work.

If you want to run this with your own team, keep it simple. 

Ask the same three questions consistently.
Listen closely. 
Write the answers down. 
Look for repetition. 

That is where you begin.

Because the best AI adoption strategy starts with listening, not deploying.

A lot of organisations are still thinking reactively when it comes to hiring AI Architects.

Even after hiring an AI Architect, the approach often remains reactive.

My advice isn’t to hire 3–4 AI Architects straight away because who has that amount of budget and capability?!

But 100% companies need to hire one strong Principal Architect and develop 2–3 architects internally into future AI Architects.

A Principal Architect brings the experience to mentor, structure, and scale a small architecture capability properly.

If you don’t build that internal capability, at some point someone will take your best AI Architect and you’re back at square one competing with the market.

If the AI Architect comes in on a contract basis, they should be building the next AI Architect internally as part of their role.

If I had to start over today with zero audience, zero clients, and zero money, here's exactly how I'd make my first $10,000 with Claude.

A few months ago, a fire inspection company paid me $5,000 to build an app completely in Claude Code.

I have zero idea how to code. I build everything with Claude using plain English.

Here's what everyone gets wrong:

Trap 1: Consumer apps (habit trackers, productivity tools)

You need 1,000 users at $10/month to hit $10K. Massive marketing budget. Brutal churn.

Trap 2: Chasing shiny new tools

New AI tool drops. Everyone makes reaction videos. Nobody makes money.

Here's the path nobody talks about:

1,000 strangers vs 4 business owners.
Consumer app: 1,000 users × $10/month = $10,000
B2B software: 4 clients × $2,500/month = $10,000
The Boring Problems Framework:
Businesses pay $10,000+ to solve these:

Manual and repetitive work
Compliance or revenue critical
Outdated software
Disconnected systems
Previously impossible (pre-AI)

Why boring businesses are gold:
→ They have money ($1M-$10M/year revenue)
→ They have real pain (manual processes killing them)
→ Nobody is building for them (AI creators chase sexy niches)

The 7-stage system:

Pick boring industry (HVAC, plumbers, dental, auto shops)
Find specific $10K+ problem
Outreach (Google Maps, Facebook groups)
Discovery call ("Walk me through your day. Where are you wasting time?")
Blueprint (Claude turns transcript into build plan)
Pricing (charge $10K-$50K based on value)
Proposal (professional scope + contract)

Real example:
Fire inspection company was hiring $50K/year person for paperwork.
Plus training. Plus benefits. Plus sick leave.
My solution: $15,000 upfront + $1,500/month.
Works 24/7. Never quits.
Why this works:
You're not selling AI.
You're selling time back.
Solving expensive, painful problems that cost real money.
Your 4 steps this week:

Pick an industry
Find 10 businesses (Google Maps)
Reach out to all 10
Build what they tell you

While everyone chases viral apps, you're signing $10,000 contracts with local businesses nobody else talks to.

1️⃣ Connect with me
2️⃣ Like this post
3️⃣ Comment "CLAUDE"

I'll send the exact framework, scripts, and Claude skills for every stage.
♻️ Repost if you're tired of chasing consumer apps

⫸ Anthropic is hiring a Transaction Principal. It's a new kind of procurement.

Read the JD. Sourcing data center capacity across FLAP-D, the Nordics, Southern Europe. RFPs. Term sheets. LOIs. Developer negotiations.
That's procurement.

But look at what else is in there:

→ Marry the right projects, capital stacks, and developers at the right stages → Navigate grid connection queues and permitting across jurisdictions 
→ Partner with the "Compute Markets Manager" on market strategy

Five jobs that used to be five careers. One role.

Three things this signals for our profession:

The category is now - capacity, not supplier. Anthropic calls it Compute Markets. 

You don't manage a vendor - you enter a market. Anyone who has sourced coffee, copper, or lithium knows this logic. Anyone who has only sourced cloud contracts is about to.

Strategic sourcing has merged with project finance. When you're structuring capital stacks, you're not buying a service - you're co-structuring your supplier's ability to deliver.

Transaction is replacing procurement as the verb. A transaction is bespoke, structured from first principles. Procurement is recurring, against a pre-qualified list. The title isn't accidental.

Procurement was designed for a world where supply was abundant and the job was to extract margin. That world is gone for the categories that matter - compute, power, critical minerals. The job now is to co-create capacity with counterparties before your competitors do.

#procurement #futureofwork #ai #technology #careers

It used to be about finding the "best athletes" for your team. Now? The first hurdle is simply proving the person on the other side of the screen is actually a human being.

I sat down to discuss a shift that is keeping CHROs up at night: AI has broken the traditional hiring contract.

At Mitek Systems, they’re seeing the front lines of this "Trust Revolution":

- Synthetic Candidates: Entirely fake resumes and profiles generated by bots to flood your pipeline.
- Deepfake Interviews: Candidates who look like a "Jeff Bell" on LinkedIn but show up as someone else entirely on Zoom.
- Laptop Farms: Fraudsters taking on multiple roles at once to collect paychecks until they are caught.

This isn’t just an "HR headache", it’s a massive security risk to your company’s IP and data.

The seismic shift Judith Ohrn Hicks says we have to make: Identity verification can’t happen after the offer anymore. To protect our teams, we have to move proof of identity to the very start of the funnel.

The "naked eye" isn't enough to catch a sophisticated deepfake anymore. It’s time we use AI to fight AI.

Have you encountered a "synthetic candidate" or a suspicious interview lately?

Noticed this lately?
Instead of interviews → offer → onboarding…

It’s now:
“Hey, can you do a small project first?”

And that “small project” is slowly becoming the hiring process.

A few things driving this shift:
- Companies got burned by bad hires (especially post-remote boom)
- Roles are getting too specialized to judge via interviews
- AI has made resumes… let’s be honest… unreliable
- Speed matters — no one wants a 3-month hiring cycle anymore

So companies are thinking:
“Let’s just see them work.”

Some numbers to ground this:
- Contract and project-based hiring has grown steadily over the last 2–3 years, especially in tech and marketing roles
- Many startups now allocate 20–40% of roles as contract-first positions before converting to full-time
- Freelance platforms and short-term staffing demand continue to rise — not because of cost, but because of certainty

And honestly, from a business perspective… it makes sense. A 2-week project tells you more than 4 rounds of interviews.

But here’s where it gets messy.
For candidates:
- You’re investing time without guarantee
- Sometimes multiple companies are asking for “test work” at the same time
- And not all of it is actually “test” — some of it is… free work

For companies:
- Great talent may walk away if the process feels exploitative
- Good candidates don’t always have time to “prove” themselves repeatedly

The interesting part?
This trend isn’t going away. If anything, it’ll become more structured:
- Paid trial projects
- Short-term contracts before full-time offers
- Role-based simulations replacing interviews

But here’s the line companies need to be careful about:
If “trial hiring” starts feeling like free labour disguised as evaluation,
people will push back. Hard.

#trialhiring #futureofwork #hiringtrends #staffaugmentation #contractjobs #talentstrategy #recruitment #hrinsights #workculture #gigwork

☕ #AI just opened a café in Sweden and it hired the staff

Last week, Andon Labs launched an AI-run store in San Francisco. This week, it opened Andon Café in #Stockholm, managed by an AI named Mona.

📍Located at #Norrbackagatan 48, Mona’s mission wasn’t just selling coffee, it was proving whether AI can run a real business across borders and navigate European bureaucracy.

What #Mona did in days:

• Applied for Swedish permits
• Signed a 3-year electricity contract
• Created fire safety documents
• Contacted local suppliers
• Designed the menu
• Interviewed and hired 2 human baristas

In just 4 days, the café reportedly made around $1,000 in sales.

Where AI still fails:

Mona ordered 120 eggs for a kitchen with no stove. When told they couldn’t be boiled, she suggested baking them in the oven. The barista replied: “They will explode.”

She also assigned midnight tasks and asked staff to buy supplies with their own money.

This isn’t about coffee. It’s about whether AI can independently operate companies, make hiring decisions, sign contracts, and manage workers.

Andon says this is a controlled experiment but it hints at a future where AI managers may become normal faster than expected.

Refold AI raised $6.5M. Co-led by Eniac Ventures and Tidal Ventures.

Their bet: AI agents that replace the system integrator layer. The work that used to take six-figure SI contracts and six-month rollouts is now handled by agents that learn, write, and maintain the integration code themselves.

We designed their website a few months back. The buyers are VPs at companies where "I brought this vendor in" is a career-shaping decision. The site can't look like an AI demo. It has to pass the shortlist test.

The shortlist test: a VP lines up 8 vendor sites at 2pm on a Wednesday, clicks through each for under 90 seconds, and decides which 3 get the demo call. If your site doesn't answer "what you do, who you do it for, and why the enterprise buyer should trust you" in that window, you're not on the shortlist.

We built Refold's site illustrative and enterprise-ready. Calm, evidence-dense, engineered to pass that test and nothing else. SaaSpo picked it up // nice side-effect. The real win: their pipeline started converting buyers who said "the site convinced my boss before the demo did."

Here's what we've learned: every enterprise website is a hiring decision. The buyer isn't buying your product - they're hiring you to be their next vendor. The site is the interview.

Congrats to Jugal Anchalia, Abhishek Kumar, Drishti Tulsi and the Refold AI team. Excited to see what's next.

AI is auditing contracts. Algorithms are shortlisting candidates. Machine learning models are approving loans.

But who's auditing the AI?

Across Africa and the Middle East, organizations are deploying AI systems faster than they're building oversight frameworks. The result? Bias baked into hiring algorithms. Credit models that perpetuate inequality. Customer service bots that alienate rather than assist.

The most forward-thinking organizations aren't just asking "Can AI do this?" They're asking "Should it?" and "How do we prove it's working fairly?"

AI governance isn't a tech problem. It's a business risk problem. And it requires professionals who can bridge the gap between data science, compliance, and strategic decision-making.

Model governance. Bias detection. Algorithmic risk assessment. These aren't buzzwords anymore. They're core competencies for auditors, compliance officers, and risk managers navigating the next decade.

The question isn't whether your organization will use AI. It's whether you'll have the expertise to ensure it's used responsibly.

Start building that capability now.

Infosys FY26 results were 💩 

For a company that calls itself “AI-first": 

> Revenue per employee is down 0.8%
> Panicked & cut 8.4k roles in Q4 
> Yet, managed to add net 11.3k headcount in FY26

There are 3 clear root causes ⤵️

1️⃣The FY26 fresher hiring gamble has backfired
- In Jan 2026 at Davos, CEO Salil Parekh publicly committed to hiring 20k college graduates in FY26; he cited "demand recovery" and "AI-led growth opportunities".
- Same week at the same event - TCS CEO K Krithivasan went the other way: "We will need a lesser proportion of programmers."
- Infosys hired 18k freshers through FY26 - then cut 8.4k roles in Q4 alone to course-correct.

2️⃣Poor productivity growth (even when adjusting for fresher over-hiring)
- On a constant currency basis, revenue per employee at Infosys is down by 0.8%
- Even if we adjust for the fact that Infosys over-hired freshers (assume ~7k still in training out of the 18k freshers hired), revenue per employee is still down by ~0.4%
- In the same period, TCS has reported 1.5% YoY growth in revenue per employee (constant currency basis)

3️⃣Lip service to AI 
- Infosys reported last quarter than AI is ~5.5% of revenue; it has declined to release the same for Q4!
- Whereas, TCS disclosed in Q4 FY26 that ~7.5% of revenue is from AI contracts ($2.3bn run rate). 

But, there is some silver lining to these results from Infosys which we have to give them credit for:
(a) Revenue growth of 3.1% for FY26 (constant currency) is much better than TCS (-2.4%) and Wipro (negative third consecutive year)
(b) EBIT margin ~21% - they have maintained operational discipline in soft revenue environment.
(c) Attrition of 12.6% is middle of the pack versus peers: TCS 13.7% > Persistent 13% > Infosys 12.6% > HCLTech 12.5% > Tech Mahindra 12.1%

Infosys management insists that it is an “AI first” company - but their Q4 FY26 results painted a starkly different story.

➡️ The company had put its best foot forward at Infosys AI Day 2026 - shareholders have to truly believe that Topaz Fabric will be able to beat out WisdomNext by TCS - else we will see increasingly divergent results!

AI flagged it as benign. The patient came back six months later with stage III cancer.

Who answers for that?

This is no longer a hypothetical. As AI tools enter pathology workflows across Indian hospitals, one question is becoming impossible to avoid:

When AI misses a cancer on a slide who is liable?

The algorithm that returned a false negative? The vendor who sold it? The pathologist who co-signed the report? Or the institution that mandated its use?

There is no clean answer. And that ambiguity is exactly what makes this the most important ethical debate in pathology right now.

Here is what we know:
→ AI in cancer diagnostics has demonstrated sensitivity rates that rival  sometimes exceed senior pathologists in controlled studies
→ But real-world performance and controlled-trial performance are very different things
→ In India, there is currently no standardised regulatory framework defining AI's legal role in histopathology sign-off
→ Most pathologists using AI tools today have not been shown a contract that explicitly reassigns liability

That last point should give every practising pathologist pause.

The tool is only as accountable as the system around it. And right now, that system is still being written.

This is not an argument against AI. Digital pathology is transforming turnaround times, enabling remote reporting, and extending specialist reach into underserved regions all of which matter enormously in a country with fewer than 12,000 pathologists.

But adoption without accountability frameworks is a risk nobody should accept quietly.

The pathologists who will lead through this transition are those who understand the technology deeply enough to interrogate it not just use it.

That expertise is increasingly what top institutions are hiring for.

At MedLink Jobs, we work with leading hospitals and diagnostic chains across India actively seeking MD / DNB Pathologists who can navigate both the science and the complexity of modern pathology practice.

If you are building that kind of career  or ready for your next role  let's connect.

Visit medlinkjobs.com or drop a message. The right opportunity should find you.

IT Sector Outlook…!!!

Why Is the IT Sector Bleeding Money? 
Let's be real.
Every week, another tech giant announces layoffs. Stock prices fall. Profits shrink.
And everyone asks — "But IT was supposed to be recession-proof?"

Here's what's actually happening 👇

𝟎𝟏 — 🔥 Over-hiring During the Pandemic Boom
Companies hired aggressively between 2020–2022 banking on infinite digital growth. That bubble burst. Now they're paying the price — literally.

𝟎𝟐 — 🤖 AI Is Replacing Repetitive Roles Faster Than Expected
Automation is not coming — it's already here. Junior dev roles, QA, data entry, basic coding tasks — AI handles them now. Companies don't need the headcount they once did.

𝟎𝟑 — 📉 Clients Are Cutting IT Budgets
In a high-interest-rate economy, every CFO is scrutinizing tech spend. Big enterprise contracts are shrinking, delayed, or cancelled entirely.

𝟎𝟒 — 🏗️ Legacy Infrastructure Eating Profits Alive
Maintaining 10-year-old systems while trying to modernize costs a fortune. Many IT firms are spending more on keeping the lights on than building the future.

𝟎𝟓 — 🌍 Global Competition + Pricing Pressure
Offshore talent markets have commoditized software services. Margins are razor-thin. What used to bill at $150/hr now competes with $15/hr alternatives.

𝟎𝟔 — 🚀 Failed Moonshots & Misallocated R&D
Companies poured billions into Metaverse, Web3, NFTs. Most failed spectacularly. That capital is gone — and the write-offs are massive.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

The IT sector isn't dying — it's RESTRUCTURING.

The companies that will win are those investing in AI-augmented teams, lean operations, and delivering real ROI — not just technical complexity.

Here's my take:

This is not a crisis for IT. It's a RESET. The professionals and companies that adapt — upskill, specialize, and stay lean — will emerge stronger than ever.

The ones clinging to the old model? They'll be the cautionary tales in next year's case studies.

What's your read on this? Drop your thoughts below.

"Combined, the three lenders employed 104,266 people at end-2025, down 2.6% from the year before"

"The combined workforce of Singapore’s three local banks shrank by nearly 3,000 roles in 2025, mirroring broader trends across the global banking sector amid restructuring and productivity efforts.

The three lenders employed 104,266 people at end-2025, down 2.6 per cent – or 2,806 employees – from 107,072 at end-2024, based on Bloomberg data and the banks’ annual reports.

DBS accounted for about three-fifths of the total decline. Its workforce fell 3.9 per cent, or 1,624 employees, to 39,721 at end-2025.

OCBC posted a fall of 1 per cent, or 333 employees, with staff strength at 33,323 at end-2025, from 33,656 a year earlier. 

UOB recorded a 2.6 per cent decrease, or 849 employees, to 31,222 at end-2025, from 32,071 the year before.

For DBS, the reduction was “mainly due to post-integration synergies in India and Taiwan” as well as natural attrition and non-renewal of contracts, a spokesperson told The Business Times.

In 2023, DBS completed its acquisition of Citigroup’s consumer banking business in Taiwan, while its India subsidiary merged with Lakshmi Vilas Bank in 2020.

“Aside from this, our employee headcount both in Singapore and overseas has remained relatively stable,” the DBS spokesperson said. In 2025, 45 per cent of its job vacancies were filled by internal candidates.

OCBC said that hiring aligned with identified growth areas, with recruitment carried out when needed. 

“With new technologies increasingly transforming the workplace, roles would naturally evolve and change,” said Ernest Phang, OCBC’s head of talent acquisition, total rewards, risk and compliance in group human resources.

UOB’s head of group human resources Dean Tong said the lender’s change in headcount “primarily reflected natural workforce movements over the course of the year”.

This is as UOB took a “disciplined approach” to workforce management amid a “more uncertain global environment”, Tong said. The approach included a “continued focus on productivity” and prioritising critical roles, while being “measured” about overall headcount growth.

AI and productivity gains

The three banks did not say whether the reductions affected temporary or permanent staff more, nor did they attribute the lower headcount directly to productivity gains from artificial intelligence.

However, analysts have warned of AI replacing human workers at global lenders, with one Bloomberg Intelligence report in 2025 saying banks worldwide could cut as many as 200,000 positions over the next three to five years."

#INFY (#Infosys) FY26 — Profitable resilience in a cautious tape. Revenue hits ₹1,78,650 Cr. Q4 PAT surges +20.9%. FY27 Guidance: 1.5%–3.5% CC.

Infosys is successfully navigating the transition to an "AI-First" model, using automation to protect margins even as the global spending environment remains hesitant. While the Q4 profit beat was aided by tax reversals, the core story is one of elite cash generation.

Key numbers:
• Annual Revenue: ₹1,78,650 Cr (+9.6% YoY)
• Q4 PAT: ₹8,501 Cr (+20.9% YoY | +27.8% QoQ)
• Free Cash Flow (FCF): $3.7 Billion (112.3% FCF conversion)
• Operating Margin: 21.0% (Strong 260 bps QoQ expansion)
• FY27 Guidance: 1.5% – 3.5% CC (Conservative outlook)

What's genuinely exciting:
✅ Cash Flow Fortress: FCF conversion at 112% of net profit. Infosys remains one of the world's most efficient machines at turning contracts into hard cash.
✅ AI-Led Efficiency: Operating margins jumped to 21% despite low growth, proving that the "Topaz" AI framework is delivering real productivity gains.
✅ Shareholder Rewards: Returned over ₹37,500 Cr via dividends and buybacks in FY26. A massive payout for a "defensive" heavyweight.
✅ BFSI Recovery: The critical Financial Services segment grew 2.9% YoY, signaling the start of a slow normalization in banking tech spend.
✅ Healthy Deal Pipeline: $14.9 Billion in TCV deal wins during the year provides a solid revenue floor.

What demands scrutiny:
❌ Conservative Guidance: The 1.5%–3.5% CC growth guide for FY27 suggests discretionary spending hasn't fully returned.
❌ Negative Sequential Growth: In constant currency (CC) terms, Q4 revenue actually declined by 1.3% QoQ.
❌ Headcount Red Flag: Headcount fell by 8,440 employees in Q4. While management cites AI productivity, three consecutive quarters of decline signal a lack of volume-led momentum.
❌ Tax-Aided Beat: Much of the Q4 PAT surge came from a one-time ₹774 Cr tax reversal, not purely operational outperformance.

The number nobody is talking about Infosys is essentially replacing human labor with AI bots to protect its 21% margin. The decline of 8,440 employees in a single quarter—while billable deals are being signed—is the clearest sign yet that the traditional "linear" hiring model of Indian IT is dead. FY27 will be the ultimate test: Can they grow the top line without growing the bench?

The thesis is compelling
Infosys remains the gold standard for "profitable resilience." It is no longer an "explosive" growth story, but an elite cash-compounding machine. With $3.7B in FCF and a debt-free balance sheet, it is the ultimate defensive play for an uncertain global macro cycle.

₹1.78 Lakh Cr Revenue. 112% FCF Conversion. AI is protecting the margin, but volume growth is waiting for a trigger.

Meta just told 8,000 people they’re the price of the future.

Not performance. Not misconduct. Just the math of a $135 billion AI bet that needed to be offset somewhere — and somewhere turned out to be human beings.

Five things worth sitting with:
𝟭. 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝘀𝘂𝗯𝘀𝘁𝗶𝘁𝘂𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗶𝘀 𝗻𝗼 𝗹𝗼𝗻𝗴𝗲𝗿 𝘁𝗵𝗲𝗼𝗿𝗲𝘁𝗶𝗰𝗮𝗹.
Zuckerberg said it plainly in January: projects that once needed big teams can now be done by one very talented person. The 10% isn’t cost-cutting. It’s a capability statement.

𝟮. 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗹𝗼𝗼𝗽 𝗶𝘀 𝗮𝗹𝗺𝗼𝘀𝘁 𝗲𝗹𝗲𝗴𝗮𝗻𝘁, 𝗶𝗻 𝗮 𝗰𝗼𝗹𝗱 𝘄𝗮𝘆.
Meta is laying off workers — then tracking the keystrokes and mouse clicks of those who remain, through something called the “Model Capability Initiative,” to train the AI agents that will reduce the need for more workers. The humans are now the training data.

𝟯. 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝟲,𝟬𝟬𝟬 𝘂𝗻𝗳𝗶𝗹𝗹𝗲𝗱 𝗿𝗼𝗹𝗲𝘀 𝘀𝗮𝘆 𝗺𝗼𝗿𝗲 𝘁𝗵𝗮𝗻 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝟴,𝟬𝟬𝟬 𝗰𝘂𝘁.
Layoffs are visible. Hiring freezes are quiet. Both point the same direction — and the freeze is the real long-term signal.

𝟰. 𝗧𝗵𝗶𝘀 𝗶𝘀𝗻’𝘁 𝗮 𝗠𝗲𝘁𝗮 𝘀𝘁𝗼𝗿𝘆. 𝗜𝘁’𝘀 𝗮 𝘁𝗲𝗺𝗽𝗹𝗮𝘁𝗲.
Amazon: 16,000. Block: 40% of workforce. Meta: 10%, or 8,000. AI efficiency is becoming the normalized reason — the one that no longer requires much explanation. The memo writes itself now.

𝟱. 𝗙𝗼𝗿 𝗜𝗻𝗱𝗼𝗻𝗲𝘀𝗶𝗮, 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝘄𝗶𝗻𝗱𝗼𝘄 𝗶𝘀 𝘀𝘁𝗶𝗹𝗹 𝗼𝗽𝗲𝗻 — 𝗯𝘂𝘁 𝗶𝘁’𝘀 𝗺𝗼𝘃𝗶𝗻𝗴.
Meta’s Indonesia footprint is not small. Facebook, Instagram, and WhatsApp are woven into how 200+ million of us communicate, shop, and run businesses — from warung owners taking orders on WhatsApp to UMKM sellers whose entire storefront is an Instagram grid. A 10% global cut means fewer hands on trust and safety, fewer eyes on local partnerships, and likely a leaner Jakarta presence serving one of Meta’s largest user markets. Our own platforms, logistics players, and fintech operators are reading the same playbook. The question was never whether this arrives here. It’s whether we build workforce resilience before it does — in policy, in education, in how we rethink the social contract between employers and the people being replaced by machines.

The optimist in me believes we still have time. The realist in me knows time is the one thing AI is compressing fastest.

But history is worth remembering. Every major technological shift — the steam engine, the internet, the smartphone — disrupted more than people expected, and created more than people feared. The recovery was always real. The difference this time is speed.

And speed requires preparation, not panic.

🚀 The Future of Work is Here — And Staffing is Leading the Charge

The technology landscape in 2026 is moving at an unprecedented pace. AI, automation, cloud, and cybersecurity aren't just buzzwords anymore — they are the backbone of every growing business. But here's the question no one is asking loudly enough:

Who ensures the right humans are behind this technology?

That's where the staffing industry steps in.

The demand for niche talent in AI, cloud, cybersecurity, and digital platforms continues to outpace supply — and staffing firms are the bridge. AI-driven tools help staffing teams evaluate skills, availability, and fit more efficiently, reducing time-to-fill and improving placement accuracy — without losing the human insight that technology alone cannot replicate.

Here's what the evolution looks like in 2026:

✅ Skills-based hiring now prioritizes proven capabilities over degrees or rigid experience requirements — opening talent pipelines that were previously locked.
✅ Employers are using AI-driven tools, digital scheduling systems, and automated onboarding to improve efficiency and reduce hiring friction — making placements faster than ever.
✅ Contract and contingent talent are key tools for navigating business cycles where short-term projects or seasonal work require rapid workforce adjustments.
.✅ AI agents in 2026 can independently manage up to 80% of transactional recruitment tasks  — freeing up recruiters to focus on relationships, culture fit, and strategic talent consulting.

The staffing industry isn't being replaced by technology. It's being supercharged by it.

The U.S. staffing sector is projected to reach $183 billion in 2026 — and the firms winning are those combining AI tools with deep human expertise.

The future belongs to those who can hire for what's next, not just what's now.

After 7+ years building native Android & iOS apps at EY, Geko, and GoMobile...

I decided to build in public.

🚀 Here's what I shipped in the last 6 months:

While searching for my next Senior Mobile role, I created 5 open-source libraries solving real problems I faced in enterprise mobile development:

📱 **FLOWLLM** - Stream LLMs on Android without boilerplate
Built because integrating Gemini/OpenAI/Claude on Android was too verbose.
One Composable. Real-time streaming. Works offline.

🧠 **XCODE-CONTEXT** - AI that understands your iOS project  
Built because Claude reads code but doesn't understand architecture.
Now it can analyze your iOS app structure, generate tests, spot memory leaks.

⚡ **SYNCWAVE** - Real-time state management (for collaborative apps)
Built because Redux doesn't work for multi-device sync.
Uses CRDTs like Figma does. Works offline. Undo/redo across devices.

🛠️ **NEXUS** - Impact analysis before deployment
Built because I spent too many 3 AM mornings debugging unexpected production issues.
Analyzes code changes, calculates ripple effects, generates release notes.

🤖 **ENVOY** - Stop writing .env boilerplate
Built because I was writing the same data 4 times (schema, types, example, docs).
One command generates all three.

---

## 💭 Why I'm Sharing This

I've been searching for the right Senior Mobile role since November.

Traditional job hunting isn't working.

But showing what I actually build? That speaks louder than any CV.

These libraries show:
✅ I identify real problems (not just code features)
✅ I ship quality, maintainable code
✅ I think across multiple domains (Android, iOS, backend, DevOps, AI)
✅ I engage with engineering communities
✅ I'm committed to solving problems, not just implementing specs

---

## 🎯 What I'm Looking For

A **Senior Mobile Engineer** role (Android/iOS or both) where I can:
- Build scalable, enterprise-grade applications
- Drive architectural decisions
- Own features end-to-end
- Collaborate with teams that value code quality
- Keep shipping & learning

**Remote preferred** | **Flexible on contract/full-time** | **UTC-6 timezone**

---

## 🔗 Check My Work

📚 **Full technical breakdowns**: dev.to/frxncisxo
🐙 **GitHub**: github.com/frxcisxo
💼 **LinkedIn**: linkedin.com/in/frxncisxo

If you're hiring or know a team that needs a builder who ships quality mobile apps, let's talk ☕

🎯 Hiring: Recruiter — AstroumAI, Hyderabad 
Astroum AI is a patent-pending AI company building BRAHMO — a knowledge infrastructure product for professionals and teams. USPTO filed. Early access launching.
We're hiring across GTM, engineering, and ops over the next 6 months.
Looking for a recruiter who can own the full hiring funnel — not just forward resumes.

🔷 WHAT YOU'LL OWN
→ First mandate: Growth & GTM Analyst role (live on my profile now). Closing target: 3 weeks.
→ Source candidates across LinkedIn, Naukri, Instahyre, AngelList, and your own network.
→ Screen applications. Filter out spray-and-pray resumes and AI-generated applications before they reach me.
→ First-round calls. 20-minute screen on experience, communication, and the work-sample task. You recommend who advances.
→ Scheduling and candidate communication end to end.
→ Offer coordination and closing — salary conversations, notice-period negotiations, references.
→ Pipeline maintenance. Even when no role is open, you keep a bench of 5–10 warm candidates per function.
→ Follow-on roles: frontend engineer, backend engineer, designer, ops associate — rolling through Q3 2026.

✅ MANDATORY
1-3+ years recruiting experience, ideally at a startup or boutique agency
Proven closes in GTM or tech roles in Hyderabad / Bangalore
Hands-on with LinkedIn Recruiter, Naukri, Instahyre
Sharp written communication — you're representing the company to candidates
Comfortable pushing back on me when a brief is unrealistic
You screen hard. Mediocre shortlists are worse than empty ones.
➕ BONUS
Prior experience hiring for B2B SaaS or AI startups
Existing network in Hyderabad tech community
You've closed your own role within 30 days at a previous gig

📋 THE ENGAGEMENT
📍 Remote, Hyderabad-based preferred 📅 3-month contract to full time hire, monthly salary 🔒 NDA before you see any material

Email hr@astroum.ai with:

Your LinkedIn profile, resume.
One short paragraph: how you'd source candidates for a founder-led GTM role at a pre-revenue AI startup where most applicants won't have heard of us.
No agency decks. No generic pitches.
Start date: within 1 week of agreement.
